questions.question: Define the `final` keyword in Java and explain its significance in OOP.
keyPoints: The final keyword restricts modification/overriding
Applied to variables, methods, and classes
Ensures immutability and security
Prevents unintended changes in code
Enforces design constraints in inheritance hierarchy

questions.question: Explain the difference between a final variable and a static final variable with an example.
keyPoints: final - instance-level constant (separate copy per object)
static final - class-level constant (one copy shared by all instances
Example: final int INSTANCE_ID vs static final int MAX_INSTANCES
static final variables can be accessed without object creation
Naming convention: final uses camelCase, static final uses UPPER_CASE

questions.question: What is a blank final variable in Java? When and how is it initialized?
keyPoints: Blank final - declared without initialization
Must be initialized before use
Can be initialized in constructor(s)
Can be initialized in instance initialization block
Each constructor must initialize it if multiple constructors exist
Gives flexibility in initializing final variables

questions.question: Differentiate between final, finally, and finalize in Java.
keyPoints: final - keyword for immutability restriction
finally - block in exception handling, always executes
finalize() - method called by garbage collector before object destruction
All three are different and serve different purposes
final is compile-time, finally is runtime, finalize is garbage collection related

questions.question: Why cannot abstract classes be declared as final in Java?
keyPoints: Abstract classes must be extended to be useful
Final classes cannot be extended
These two concepts are mutually exclusive
Compiler error if both are used
Abstract class provides template, final class prevents extension - contradictory

questions.question: Explain the effect of declaring a method as final in Java.
keyPoints: Method cannot be overridden by subclasses
Provides guarantee that method behavior won't change
Used for security or design stability
Method can still be overloaded (different parameters)
Method is inherited but behavior is fixed

questions.question: What is the naming convention for final variables in Java and why is it important?
keyPoints: Use UPPER_CASE with underscores
Example: MAX_SIZE, PI_VALUE
Distinguishes constants from regular variables
Follows JavaBeans naming guidelines
Improves code readability and maintainability
Helps developers identify constants instantly

questions.question: Explain in detail the three different contexts in which the `final` keyword can be applied in Java: variables, methods, and classes. Provide suitable examples for each.
keyPoints: Variables: final int x = 10; - cannot reassign after initialization, becomes constant
Methods: final void display() {} - cannot override in subclasses
Classes: final class String {} - cannot extend
Blank final variables - initialized in constructor
Final parameters - cannot modify in method
Static final for class-level constants
Use cases: security, immutability, design constraints
Real-world Java classes: String is final, Math class has final methods

questions.question: Write a Java program demonstrating the use of final with variables (including blank final), final methods, and final classes. Explain each part of the program.
keyPoints: Program showing: final variable initialization
Program showing: blank final variable initialized in constructor
Program showing: final method that cannot be overridden
Program showing: final class that cannot be extended
Proper explanation of each concept
Compilation and execution results
Common errors and how to avoid them

questions.question: Discuss the advantages of using the `final` keyword in Java programming. How does it contribute to writing efficient and secure code?
keyPoints: Immutability guarantees - prevents accidental modifications
Thread safety - final variables are inherently thread-safe
Performance optimization - compiler can make optimizations
Design stability - final methods/classes prevent unwanted changes
Security - prevents extension of security-critical classes
Code clarity - clearly expresses developer intent
Prevents inheritance abuse - controls extensibility
Documentation value - signals unchangeable elements

questions.question: What are the differences between constant in C++ and final constant in Java? How does Java handle constants compared to other programming languages?
keyPoints: Java: static final for compile-time constants
C++: const for runtime, #define for compile-time
Java has no preprocessor macros
Java final is more restrictive than C++ const
Java constants must be initialized at declaration or static block
Interface variables are implicitly final in Java
No mutable constants in Java unlike some languages
Comparison with const in C/C++, val in Scala
